title: Dependency Injection Deep Dive
summary: Bean lifecycle, scopes, proxy types, circular dependencies, and how Spring's IoC container really works.

sections.content:
## The IoC Container

Spring's core is the **Inversion of Control (IoC) container** — rather than objects creating their dependencies, the container creates and injects them. This decouples components and makes testing easy.

Two main container types:
- `BeanFactory` — lazy, basic DI
- `ApplicationContext` — eager (by default), adds events, i18n, AOP, auto-detection

sections.title: Bean Lifecycle
sections.content:
graph LR
    A[Class discovered\n@Component scan] --> B[BeanDefinition created]
    B --> C[Bean instantiated\nconstructors called]
    C --> D[Dependencies injected\n@Autowired]
    D --> E[BeanPostProcessor\nbeforeInitialization]
    E --> F[@PostConstruct\nInitializingBean.afterPropertiesSet]
    F --> G[BeanPostProcessor\nafterInitialization]
    G --> H[Bean Ready for Use]
    H --> I[Context closes]
    I --> J[@PreDestroy\nDisposableBean.destroy]
    style H fill:#059669,color:#fff
    style J fill:#dc2626,color:#fff

sections.title: Prototype in Singleton Problem
sections.content: When you inject a prototype-scoped bean into a singleton, you get the same prototype instance every time — defeating the purpose. Solutions: (1) Inject ApplicationContext and call getBean() manually, (2) Use @Lookup method injection, (3) Use ObjectProvider<T>.

sections.title: Proxy Types: JDK Dynamic vs CGLIB
sections.content:
graph TD
    subgraph JDK Dynamic Proxy
        I[Interface] --> P1[JDK Proxy]
        P1 --> Bean1[Real Bean]
        Note1[Bean must implement interface]
    end
    subgraph CGLIB Proxy
        P2[CGLIB Subclass] --> Bean2[Real Bean]
        Note2[Works on any class\nbut cannot proxy final]
    end
    Spring[Spring decides which to use] --> I
    Spring --> P2
    style P1 fill:#4f46e5,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#059669,color:#fff

sections.question: Why is constructor injection preferred over field injection in Spring?
options: Constructor injection is faster at runtime
Field injection creates circular dependencies more often
Constructor injection makes dependencies explicit, allows final fields, and makes unit testing easy without Spring
sections.explanation: Constructor injection is preferred because: dependencies are explicit (visible in the constructor), fields can be final (immutable), and unit tests don't need Spring — just call new Service(mockDep1, mockDep2). Field injection hides dependencies and requires Spring's reflection-based injection even in tests.
